figures.gloss: The Baptist-trained catechist (c. 1887–1951) who, on 6 April 1921 at Nkamba, laid hands on a dying woman and ignited a mass healing revival that emptied mission churches and colonial work-gangs alike. Arrested within months, sentenced to death by a Belgian court-martial, commuted to life by King Albert I, he spent thirty years in Elisabethville prison and never preached again — and the movement grew anyway.
figures.facet: A church built almost entirely on its founder's ABSENCE: five months of public ministry, three decades of silence in a cell — the imprisonment itself became the passion narrative that organized the faith.
figures.provenance: Marie-Louise Martin, 'Kimbangu: An African Prophet and His Church' (1975); colonial trial records via Susan Asch, 'L'Église du prophète Kimbangu' (1983).

figures.gloss: Kimbangu's home village in Bas-Congo, renamed Nkamba-Jerusalem — the holy city where the first healing occurred, where his body was returned in 1960 and enshrined in a mausoleum, and where pilgrims draw sanctified water from the sacred pool and carry Nkamba's earth home.
figures.facet: A New Jerusalem sited not in the colonial capital but in the prophet's own village: the movement relocated the center of the Christian world to Congolese soil, and the pilgrimage economy of water and earth makes that relocation physical.
figures.provenance: Martin (1975); Susan Asch (1983) on the mausoleum, pool, and pilgrimage practice.

figures.gloss: Kimbangu's youngest son (1918–1992), the movement's chief architect: he consolidated the clandestine, persecuted networks into the Église de Jésus-Christ sur la Terre par son envoyé spécial Simon Kimbangu (EJCSK), won Belgian legal recognition in 1959, and led the church into the World Council of Churches in 1969 — the first African-initiated church admitted.
figures.facet: The institutionalizer as second founder: the prophet supplied the passion, the son supplied the church — a division of labor between charisma and organization visible within a single family.
figures.provenance: Martin (1975); Asch (1983) on the EJCSK's legalization and WCC admission.

figures.gloss: Kimbangu was received not as a novelty but as a ngunza — the Kongo prophet-healer seized and shaken by the Spirit (the trembling called zakama), who heals, exposes witchcraft, and speaks for the unseen. After 1921 the colonial state deported thousands of 'Ngunzists,' and the prophetic current repeatedly overflowed the church that tried to contain it.
figures.facet: The office is older than the movement: Kimbanguism is legible precisely because the ngunza role pre-existed it in Kongo religion — a Christian revival poured into an indigenous prophetic mold.
figures.provenance: Wyatt MacGaffey, 'Modern Kongo Prophets: Religion in a Plural Society' (1983); Martin (1975) on the deportations.

figures.gloss: Kimbanguist devotion progressively elevated the founder from 'special envoy' of Christ to incarnation of the Holy Spirit, and popular doctrine identifies his three sons with the persons of the Trinity; the church celebrates Christmas on 25 May, the birthday of the second son, Salomon Dialungana Kiangani, identified with the returned Christ. This teaching is internally central and ecumenically contested — it has strained the church's standing with the World Council of Churches.
figures.facet: A live case of divinization happening in documented time: the archive lets you watch a historical prisoner become a person of the Trinity within a single century — the process most traditions only preserve as a finished result.
figures.provenance: Anne Mélice's studies of Kimbanguist doctrine (2000s); Asch (1983); the 25 May Christmas observance is EJCSK practice, widely documented.

figures.gloss: Simon Kimbangu's wife, who led the persecuted faithful clandestinely from his 1921 arrest until her own death in 1959, sustaining the movement's networks through the founder's thirty years of imprisonment and silence. She is venerated by the EJCSK as the church's spiritual mother.
figures.facet: The name behind the church's 'built on absence' facet: if Kimbangu's imprisonment is the passion narrative, Marie Muilu is the one who actually held the congregation together through it — continuity supplied by the person history most often erases from a persecuted movement's record.
figures.provenance: Marie-Louise Martin, 'Kimbangu: An African Prophet and His Church' (1975).

figures.gloss: Kimbangu's second son (1916-2001), guardian of Nkamba after his father's death, whose 25 May birthday is kept by the EJCSK as its Christmas and who is identified in popular Kimbanguist doctrine with the returned Christ.
figures.facet: A living calendar entry: the church did not commemorate an ancient nativity but installed a documented 20th-century birthdate as its Christmas, making the incarnation claim traceable to a civil register rather than to legend.
figures.provenance: Anne Mélice's studies of Kimbanguist doctrine; Susan Asch, 'L'Église du prophète Kimbangu' (1983); the 25 May observance is standard, widely documented EJCSK practice.

figures.gloss: Former Salvation Army catechist who founded the Mission des Noirs ('Khakism') in 1939, a militant black-church current claiming Kimbangu's prophetic mantle independent of the EJCSK hierarchy; repeatedly imprisoned by the colonial state for it.
figures.facet: The concrete case behind an abstraction: the roster's own account of ngunzism describes a prophetic current that 'repeatedly overflowed the church' without naming anyone — Mpadi is that overflow, a rival prophet the movement's own success could not contain within one institution.
figures.provenance: Wyatt MacGaffey, 'Modern Kongo Prophets: Religion in a Plural Society' (1983).

figures.gloss: Competitive collective fundraising offerings — congregational groups vying publicly in giving — that finance Kimbanguist construction projects, from the Nkamba temple onward.
figures.facet: Devotion made countable and comparative: nsinsani turns generosity into a public, group-versus-group performance, so that the church's material expansion is visibly funded stone by counted contribution rather than by anonymous tithe.
figures.provenance: Susan Asch, 'L'Église du prophète Kimbangu' (1983), on EJCSK fundraising practice.

edges.note: The prophet and his holy city are one cult: the 1921 healing, the 1960 return of the body, and the mausoleum bind Kimbangu permanently to Nkamba-Jerusalem.

GOLD SEAM (substrate): Kimbangu ordered minkisi destroyed while assuming the very healing office the nganga and ngunza had filled — MacGaffey documents the Kongo prophet as the power-object's iconoclast successor.

The pilgrimage to the prophet's enshrined body and the earth of his village resonates with Kongo devotion to the powerful dead — a structural echo the church itself would reject as ancestor-cult, flagged accordingly.
GOLD SEAM: the church's own name styles Kimbangu Christ's 'special envoy,' and his arrest, colonial trial, and thirty-year passion are read liturgically through the trial of Christ.
GOLD SEAM: Kimbanguist doctrine maps the founder and his three sons onto the Trinity — an attested, internally central, ecumenically contested re-inhabiting of Nicene structure.

Colonial-era prophets whose healing revivals were answered with state suppression — mass deportations in the Congo, massacre at Wounded Knee — the same collision of indigenous prophecy and settler power.

Prophet-healers raised within three years of each other (Rātana 1918, Kimbangu 1921), each founding an independent indigenous church under a settler or colonial state — Rātana channelled his into parliament where Kimbangu's grew through its founder's imprisonment.
Each movement built its own holy capital — Rātana Pā with its twin-towered temple, Nkamba-Jerusalem with its mausoleum and pool — the prophet's home village promoted to centre of the religious world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two prophets seized bodily by a spirit/God who built a lasting religious monument and body of teaching under conditions of colonial-era pressure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: indigenous prophets of the colonial era repeatedly arrested and exiled by the colonial power for preaching the imminent reversal of its order.
Death-and-return founding prophets under colonial-era state pressure whose churches, unlike Wovoka's dance, survived into living institutions.
Husband and wife carrying two halves of one movement: his passion of imprisonment, her thirty-year clandestine leadership of the faithful he could no longer address directly.
Dialungana is the concrete case the Divinized Line entry already describes in the abstract: the second son whose birthday is the movement's Christmas and who is identified with the returned Christ.
Mpadi's Mission des Noirs is the named, historical instance of the ngunza prophetic current 'overflowing the church' that the ngunzism entry describes without naming anyone.
The competitive giving of nsinsani is what physically built and maintains Nkamba-Jerusalem's temple and mausoleum — devotional practice as construction finance.

Loose: both make competitive, public giving a mechanism of religious status and communal finance — but the Pacific Northwest potlatch redistributes rank through destruction and largesse, where nsinsani is congregational fundraising by comparison. Shared shape of 'giving as public contest,' different stakes and machinery. Flagged.

Kimbangu's ngunza revival positioned itself as healer-diviner within the nganga's older office while explicitly ordering minkisi destroyed — the prophet as the nganga's iconoclast successor, not his continuator (MacGaffey).

Two Kongo-region prophets three centuries apart, each claiming a special Christian-inflected calling, each preaching a Kongo-centered faith, and each destroyed or imprisoned by the ruling power of the day — Kimpa Vita burned by the Kongo court, Kimbangu jailed by the Belgian colonial state.
Twentieth-century founder-prophets each elevated to divine or quasi-divine status within their own movement's cosmology, alongside a separate supreme figure.
Prophet-successors who each built an independent community under a settler or colonial state and were imprisoned for it — Maungapōhatu raided in 1916, Kimbangu imprisoned from 1921.

dossier.essence: Kimbanguism is the African-initiated church born from Simon Kimbangu's healing ministry at Nkamba in the Belgian Congo — five months of public preaching in 1921 before his arrest, court-martial, and thirty-year imprisonment — and organized under his sons as the Église de Jésus-Christ sur la Terre par son envoyé spécial Simon Kimbangu (EJCSK), legally recognized in 1959 and admitted to the World Council of Churches in 1969. Centered in the DR Congo with congregations across Central Africa and the diaspora, it counts its members in the millions and holds Kimbangu to be God's special envoy — in developed church doctrine, the incarnation of the Holy Spirit.
dossier.cosmos: The frame is Christian monotheism relocated onto Congolese ground: Nkamba, the prophet's village, is Nkamba-Jerusalem, the holy city where his body lies enshrined and where sacred history now centers. Doctrine progressively divinized the founder's line, identifying Kimbangu with the Holy Spirit and his three sons with the persons of the Trinity — the church celebrates Christmas on 25 May, birthday of the second son, Salomon Dialungana Kiangani — a teaching that has strained its ecumenical standing. The dead await resurrection in broadly Christian terms, but Kongo devotion to the powerful dead shadows the practice: pilgrims visit the mausoleum and carry home Nkamba's water and earth.
dossier.practice: Practice is marked by strict discipline: prohibitions on alcohol and tobacco, monogamy, non-violence maintained through decades of colonial persecution and mass deportations, and shoes removed on Nkamba's holy ground. Pilgrimage to Nkamba, immersion in and drinking from its sacred pool, and blessed water anchor the ritual economy, while worship runs on a large hymnody the church holds to have been received in dreams and visions rather than composed.
dossier.canon: The Bible is the scripture, read in Kikongo, Lingala, and French; alongside it stands the church's corpus of spirit-received hymns and the remembered words and passion of the founder — a functional second tier of oral and sung material.
dossier.reflects: How a church can crystallize around an absence — five months of ministry and thirty years of prison silence made the founder's suppression itself the passion narrative that organized the faith.
sources: Marie-Louise Martin, 'Kimbangu: An African Prophet and His Church' (1975)
Wyatt MacGaffey, 'Modern Kongo Prophets: Religion in a Plural Society' (1983)
